coredata.dc:title: In-situ constructed solvated carbon quantum dot clusters to achieve uniform Zn plating in aqueous zinc ion batteries
coredata.dc:creator: Wang, Dengke
Zhao, Danyang
Chen, Mingjun
Zheng, Haoyuan
Yu, Jiaqiang
Qin, Xixian
Zhang, Wenming
Zhu, Qiancheng
coredata.dc:description: Zinc ion batteries (ZIBs) have become the most competitive candidates for the next-generation energy storage systems due to its low-price, good safety and high theoretical capacity. However, the uncontrollable dendrite growth and parasitic side reactions still hinder the practical application of the zinc ion batteries. Herein, a hybrid functionalized carbon quantum dots (FCQDs)/ZnSO4 electrolyte is in-situ synthesized via a simple one-pot hydrothermal method with glucose as precursor. The created FCQDs possess abundant anionic groups like hydroxyl, carboxyl and sulfonic acid groups, and those anionic groups can strongly absorb Zn2+ ions and then the FCQDs, Zn2+ and water molecules together generate a special solvation structure of solvated carbon quantum dot clusters [FCQDsZnx(H2O)y]2x+. The solvated FCQDs clusters can help to in-situ induce the uniform Zn deposition through a co-plating of Zn atoms and the functionalized FCQDs. Additionally, this unique solvation structure promotes easier dissociation of solvation water molecules, thus decreasing the de-solvation energy. The lower de-solvation energy barrier can help to restrain the water decomposition and the related by-products. Benefiting from the construction of this unique solvated FCQDs clusters, the hybrid electrolyte with FCQDs endows a better performance of both Zn||Zn symmetric cells and full ZIBs.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Zinc ion battery
Zn anode
Dendrites
Solvated clusters

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Effects of the FCQDs on Zn2+ diffusion/plating chemistry
Dendrites and by-products investigation of Zn electrode with different electrolytes

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: Nature Science Foundation of Hebei Province
xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: Natural Science Foundation of Hebei Province

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was supported by Nature Science Foundation of Hebei Province E2022201017 and the Advanced Talents Incubation Program of the Hebei University No. 521100221039.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic diagrams for Zn deposition in 2 M ZnSO4 electrolyte and compound electrolyte with FCQDs, respectively. (a) Severe metal corrosion, HER and uncontrollable dendritic growth occur when striping/plating in the 2 M ZnSO4 electrolyte. (b) The formation of dendrites and by-products are greatly inhibited in compound electrolyte due to easier de-solvation and co-deposition fueled by FCQDs.

Synthesis and characterizations of FCQDs. (a) The synthesis process of FCQDs. (b) TEM image of FCQDs. (c) HRTEM image of FCQDs. (d) AFM image and height profile of FCQDs (inserted in d). (e) FTIR spectra of FCQDs. High resolution XPS analyses for (f) C 1s, and (g) O 1s and (h) S 2p of FCQDs, respectively.

Effects of the FCQDs on Zn2+ diffusion/plating chemistry. TG and DTG profiles of FCQDs without (a) and with ZnSO4 (b). (c) Zeta potential of different solutions. (d) 1H NMR spectra of different electrolytes. (e) 17O NMR spectra of different electrolytes. (f) The adsorption energies of Zn2+ with –SO3 -, -COO- and H2O molecules. (g) MESP mapping of the FCQDs. (h) Charge density difference. (i) Simulations of electric field distribution. (j) Zn2+ flux of Zn electrodes with (right in (i, j)) and without (left in (i, j)) FCQDs.

Effect of FCQDs on the electrochemical performances of Zn electrode. (a) CA tests of ZnSO4 electrolyte and compound electrolyte with FCQDs. (b) Tafel polarization curves of Zn foil in different electrolytes. (c) Zn deposition curves of different electrolytes in Zn||Cu cells at 1 mA cm−2. (d) Rate capability of Zn||Zn symmetric cell using different electrolytes. (e) The limit current densities of different electrolytes. (f) Comparison of long-term charging/discharging of Zn symmetric cells (1 mA cm−2, 1 mAh cm−2). (g) Comparison of activation energies of different electrolytes.

Dendrites and by-products investigation of Zn electrode with different electrolytes. (a) SEM image of Zn anode after 50 cycles at 1 mA cm−2 without FCQDs. (b) SEM image of Zn anode after 50 cycles at 1 mA cm−2 with FCQDs. (c) AFM image of Zn anode after 50 cycles without FCQDs. (d) AFM image of Zn anode after 50 cycles with FCQDs. (e) XRD patterns of Zn anode after 50 cycles in ZnSO4 electrolyte and compound electrolyte with FCQDs. (f) Raman spectrum after cycling in ZnSO4 electrolyte and compound electrolyte with FCQDs and the corresponding Raman mapping (g, h). (i) XPS spectra of C 1s of Zn symmetric cell after 50 cycles under Ar + sputtering for 90 s. (j) AC-TEM image of co-deposition of Zn2+ ions and FCQDs. (k) corresponding element mapping images.

The electrochemical performance of Zn||MnO2 full cells. (a) Self-discharge test of ZnSO4 electrolyte after a rest of 48 h. (b) Self-discharge test of ZnSO4 electrolyte with FCQDs after a rest of 48 h. (c) CV curves of Zn||MnO2 with different electrolytes. (d) Rate performance of the Zn||MnO2 at different current densities from 0.1 to 2 A g−1. (e) Long-term charging/discharging tests at 1 A g−1 with different electrolytes. (f) Galvanostatic charge/discharge curves of different electrolytes at 1 A g−1. (g) Cycling performance with the loading of 9.2 mg cm−2. (h) An LED array powered by pouch cells.

head.ce:title: In-situ constructed solvated carbon quantum dot clusters to achieve uniform Zn plating in aqueous zinc ion batteries

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: National & Local Joint Engineering Laboratory of New Energy Photoelectric Devices, College of Physics Science and Technology, Hebei University, Baoding, 071002, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: National & Local Joint Engineering Laboratory of New Energy Photoelectric Devices
College of Physics Science and Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: National & Local Joint Engineering Laboratory of New Energy Photoelectric Devices, College of Physics Science and Technology, Hebei University, Baoding 071002, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Sciences, Hebei Agriculture University, Baoding, 071001, China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: College of Sciences, Hebei Agriculture University, Baoding 071001, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Zinc ion batteries (ZIBs) have become the most competitive candidates for the next-generation energy storage systems due to its low-price, good safety and high theoretical capacity. However, the uncontrollable dendrite growth and parasitic side reactions still hinder the practical application of the zinc ion batteries. Herein, a hybrid functionalized carbon quantum dots (FCQDs)/ZnSO4 electrolyte is in-situ synthesized via a simple one-pot hydrothermal method with glucose as precursor. The created FCQDs possess abundant anionic groups like hydroxyl, carboxyl and sulfonic acid groups, and those anionic groups can strongly absorb Zn2+ ions and then the FCQDs, Zn2+ and water molecules together generate a special solvation structure of solvated carbon quantum dot clusters [FCQDsZnx(H2O)y]2x+. The solvated FCQDs clusters can help to in-situ induce the uniform Zn deposition through a co-plating of Zn atoms and the functionalized FCQDs. Additionally, this unique solvation structure promotes easier dissociation of solvation water molecules, thus decreasing the de-solvation energy. The lower de-solvation energy barrier can help to restrain the water decomposition and the related by-products. Benefiting from the construction of this unique solvated FCQDs clusters, the hybrid electrolyte with FCQDs endows a better performance of both Zn||Zn symmetric cells and full ZIBs.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Nowadays, lithium-ion batteries (LIBs) have been widely used as energy storage systems for a long time, particularly in the portable electronic devices and electric vehicles due to their high energy density, long cycle life, and low self-discharge rate [1]. However, it has been plagued due to its high price, electrolyte leakage and poor safety [2,3]. Therefore, it is exceedingly necessary to develop safe and sustainable electric energy storage devices [4]. Metallic zinc is considered as a promising anode for next generation energy storage systems owing to the natural abundance of Zn resources [5], excellent cycling performance [6], high theoretical capacity (820 mAh g−1 and 5854 mAh cm−3) and environmental benignity [7,8].

Unfortunately, the uncontrollable dendrite growth and side reactions still hinder the further development of aqueous zinc ion batteries (AZIBs) [9], which mainly results from the inhomogeneous Zn deposition and the strong interaction between Zn2+ and active H2O molecules from Zn2+ solvation structure. Typically, these active H2O molecules from solvation shell have a higher priority for decomposition, which can result in hydrogen evolution reaction (HER) and side reactions [10–12]. Under such circumstance, strategies on regulating Zn2+ solvation shell have drawn tremendous attentions and fruitful achievements have been made [13,14]. On the one hand, directly reducing the amount of H2O molecules is an effective way to modulate the solvation structure of Zn2+, such as utilizing ionic liquids (ILs) [15,16], applying high concentration electrolytes [17,18] and developing solid state electrolyte [19,20]. ILs have been regarded as promising liquid solvents for batteries for they do not contain water molecules, which is conducive to eliminating HER and side reactions. In addition, high concentration electrolytes can decrease the proportion of H2O molecules, therefore the H2O molecules of [Zn(H2O)6]2+ can be replaced by anions. For example, in 30 M ZnCl2 electrolyte, the coordination of H2O and Zn2+ is greatly suppressed and the H2O molecules of [Zn(H2O)6]2+ are replaced by Cl− ions [21], thus forming a new solvation structure [Zn(H2O)Cl4]2-. Furthermore, solid-state electrolytes can also overcome the problems of side reactions caused by H2O molecules due to the water-free environment [22]. Nevertheless, ILs and high concentration electrolytes always increase the cost, which defeats the purpose of low-cost zinc-ion batteries. While, the preparation process of solid electrolyte is very complicated, and the ionic conductivity is much lower than that in liquid electrolyte. On the other hand, electrolyte additives seem to be an ideal way to inhibit dendrites and by-products by affecting the solvation structure of Zn2+ ions [23]. The introduction of appropriate amount of molecule additives can replace the water molecules around the Zn2+, forming a special solvation structure, which is beneficial to promoting the uniform deposition of Zn and inhibiting the side reactions. Although electrolyte additive is a facile way to realize the suppression of Zn dendrites and by-products. To realize the effective modulation on Zn2+ solvation structure, a widely used strategy is to utilize high concentration of additives, which can change the Zn2+ solvation structure by replacing the H2O molecules of [Zn(H2O)6]2+. However, high concentration additives still increase production costs and also affect the ionic conductivity of the electrolytes [24]. Thus, the regulation of solvation structure should be further explored, since it is directly related with water decomposition, side reactions and Zn dendrites.

Herein, we proposed a one-pot hydrothermal method to synthesize a functionalized carbon quantum dots (FCQDs)/ZnSO4 hybrid electrolyte to restrain the formation of dendrites and occurrence of side reactions by adding only 0.01 M glucose as precursors. In the hydrothermal process, glucose molecules can change into negatively charged FCQDs with abundant hydroxyl, carboxyl and sulfonic acid groups. Zinc ions tend to be strongly connected with FCQDs due to the higher adsorption energy between Zn2+ and these negatively charged groups of FCQDs than H2O molecules. Thus, compared with the traditional solvation structure of [Zn(H2O)6]2+, the FCQDs, Zn2+ ions and H2O molecules together generate a new solvation structure of solvated carbon quantum dot clusters ([FCQDsZnx(H2O)y]2x+). As illustrated in Fig. 1 , the solvated FCQDs clusters can promote the co-deposition of Zn2+ ions and FCQDs, in which FCQDs can in-situ serve as a nucleation center to induce the uniform deposition of Zn atoms. Additionally, benefiting from this unique solvation structure, the dissociation of solvation water molecules is easier than that for traditional solvated structure, thus rendering lower de-solvation energy. The lower de-solvation energy barrier can help to restrain the water decomposition and the related by-products.

A 2 M ZnSO4 electrolyte was prepared using ZnSO4·7H2O and deionized water. For compound electrolyte with FCQDs, 2 M ZnSO4·7H2O was dissolved in solvent with different weight ratio of H2O and glucose. The obtained solution was stirred for 30 min and pure into a Teflon-lined stainless-steel autoclave at 160 for 8 h. Subsequently, the reactant solution can be directly used as electrolyte with FCQDs. According to the test results, 0.01 M glucose shows better performance than other concentrations. Subsequently, FCQDs in 0.01 M glucose were collected by centrifugation, whose concentration is about 1.6 mg/mL.

The microscopic morphology of FCQDs was observed by transmission electron microscopy (TEM). X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) measurement was carried out to probe the surface compositions of FCQDs. Scanning electron microscopy (SEM) measurement was carried out to detect the surface topography. The surface topography of various modified Zn electrodes before and after cycling were performed by atomic force microscope (AFM). X-ray diffraction (XRD) was characterized using a Philips X'pert PRO diffractor with Cu Kα radiation. Fourier transform infrared spectrometer (FTIR) was tested to confirm the groups. Raman spectrum was used to identify the composition of FCQDs and the by-product of cycled Zn anode.

The Zn symmetric cells and full batteries were assembled using coin cells (CR2032) with glass fiber as separator, 2 M ZnSO4 and 2 M ZnSO4 as electrolytes respectively. The battery performance was tested using a Neware battery test system under room temperature in our lab. The Zn2+ plating/stripping Coulombic efficiencies were measured using asymmetric Zn||Cu cells with a cut-off charging potential of 1 V. The corrosion current density was obtained by a linear fitting of Tafel plots using a three-electrode configuration in 2 M ZnSO4 electrolyte and compound electrolyte with FCQDs. The activation energy was calculated from EIS data. (Chronoamperometry) CA and EIS all were carried out on electrochemical workstation (CHI660E). The cyclic voltammetry (CV) measurement was conducted on a CHI660E electrochemical workstation in the voltage range of 0.8–1.8 V (MnO2).

All of the geometries of the model complexes were fully optimized using the density functional theory (DFT) method based on the hybrid of Becke's three-parameter exchange functional and the Lee, Yang, and Parr correlation functional (B3LYP). The 6-311G（d,p）basis set was used for C H O atoms and LANL2DZ for Zn. All the calculations were performed using GAUSSIAN 16 program package.

The adsorption energies were evaluated with the following formula:
Eads = E(complex) − E(Zn2+) − E(sub)
where the first term, E(complex), is the total energy of the substrate interacting with the Zn2+. In order to evaluate the interaction between the complex, we eliminated the basis set superposition errors (BSSE) using the full counterpoise correction method.

COMSOL Multiphysics software was used to simulate the electric field distribution and Zn2+ ion flux at the electrode-electrolyte interface. The simulation used Butler−Volmer expression to describe the reaction kinetics of all electrodes and the flux of each ion was calculated by the Nernst−Planck formulation. In the simplified 2D model cell, the length and width are set to 5 and 4 μm, respectively, and the humps (0.35 μm in height) represent the initial Zn grains. The circle array (0.1 μm in radius) represents the quantum dot layer. The lower boundary of the model was set as the ground (zero V). The initial Zn2+ cation concentration was set to 2500 mol/m3.

As illustrated in Fig. 2 a, FCQDs were synthesized via a typical one-pot hydrothermal method by using a hybrid solution of 2 M ZnSO4 and 0.01 M glucose. In the hydrothermal process, the glucose molecules in-situ transformed into FCQDs in the solution. The mixture consisting ZnSO4 and FCQDs can be used as compound electrolyte directly. Digital photograph of electrolytes with various concentrations was displayed in Fig. S1. The transparent yellow solution indicates the generation of FCQDs. The TEM images of the FCQDs are exhibited in Fig. 2b. FCQDs originating from glucose with diameter of 2–6 nm can be observed and are uniformly dispersed. The high-resolution TEM (HRTEM) image reveals a good crystallinity of the FCQDs (Fig. 2c), also indicating the conversion of glucose to FCQDs after hydrothermal reaction. It is worth noting that the amorphous layer outside the FCQDs should be attributed to the functional groups. Furthermore, TEM images with different magnifications of FCQDs show the fact that FCQDs are uniform in size and evenly distributed (Fig. S2). Subsequently, AFM was used to detect the morphology of FCQDs (Fig. 2d). The height curve of line A-B shows that the height of the FCQDs ranges from 2 to 6 nm (inserted in Fig. 2d).

As shown in Fig. 2e, FTIR was utilized to identify the surface functional groups of FCQDs. These strong peaks situated at 3208 cm−1 (O–H stretching vibration), 1346 cm−1 (SO stretching vibration in sulfonic acid groups) and 1604 cm−1 (CO stretching vibration in carboxyl groups) are characteristics of FCQDs, indicating that the FCQDs possess hydroxyl [25], carboxyl [26], and sulfonic acid groups [27,28]. These results prove the existence of hydroxyl, carboxyl and sulfonic acid functional groups. Additionally, Raman spectrum was adopted to analyze the as-prepared FCQDs. It clearly showcases two obvious peaks representing G-band (well-ordered carbon) and D-band (disordered carbon), respectively (Fig. S3) [29,30]. The detailed composition of FCQDs was further analyzed via XPS measurement (Fig. 2f–h). And the presence of C 1s, O 1s and S 2p can be confirmed by the wide range of XPS results (Fig. S4). The high-resolution spectrum of C 1s exhibits four peaks, which are assigned to C–C (284.8 eV), C–O (286.3 eV), CO (288.1 eV) and C–S (289.3 eV) [31]. The spectrum of O 1s shows evident CO (530.9 eV), C–O (532.1 eV), O–H (533.4 eV) and OS (534.0 eV) components [32], which is in line with the FTIR spectra results. In the S 2p spectrum, two major peaks at 168.4 and 169.6 eV assigned to C–S and SO can be observed. The FTIR and XPS results jointly validate the existence of sulfonic acid groups and hydroxyl groups, which indicates the FCQDs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effects of the FCQDs on Zn2+ diffusion/plating chemistry
ce:section.ce:para: The thermogravimetric analysis was utilized to further analyze the surface groups and composition of FCQDs. TG and DTG curves of common FCQDs (hydrothermal reaction without ZnSO4) can be seen in Fig. 3 a, and only a peak can be observed at around 393.8 °C assigned to the typical carboxyl groups [33]. As for the FCQDs, three peaks at around 251.8 °C, 389.7 °C, 544.6 °C corresponding to hydroxyl, carboxyl and sulfonic acid groups can be seen in Fig. 3b, respectively, which reconfirms the existence of the carboxyl groups, hydroxyl groups and sulfonic acid groups on the surface of FCQDs [34]. The charge distribution of FCQDs was further examined by zeta potential, FCQDs in distilled water exhibits an extremely lower negative potential (−26.5 mV) due to their tremendous negatively charged groups (-SO3 −, -COO- and –OH). After introducing FCQDs into ZnSO4 electrolyte, the FCQDs can bind a certain number of Zn2+ ions in the electrolyte, resulting in the zeta potential changes from −26.5 mV to −7.75 mV. The change in zeta potential confirms the strong adsorption between FCQDs and Zn2+ ions (Fig. 3c) [35].

Moreover, FTIR spectra and nuclear magnetic resonance (NMR) spectra were performed to prove the interaction between FCQDs and H2O molecules. The stretching vibration of O–H shows blue-shift due to the presence of FCQDs (Fig. S5), which means the strong interaction between H2O molecules and FCQDs. As presented in Fig. 3d, the 1H peak in ZnSO4 liquid is typically located at ∼4.71 ppm, while the peak in compound electrolyte liquid with FCQDs shifts to up field, indicative of increased electronic density, which is attributed to the breakage of hydrogen bonding between H2O molecules and the stronger hydrogen bonding interactions between FCQDs and H2O molecules [36,37]. Additionally, the up-field shift of 17O NMR signal from H2O occurs after the introduction of FCQDs, further suggesting that the interactions between FCQDs and H2O molecules is strengthened (Fig. 3e). The formation of hydrogen bonds between FCQDs and H2O molecules can break the solvation structure of hydrated Zn2+, thus easily remove the solvated water molecules and decrease the de-solvation energy barrier for fast Zn||Zn2+ reversibility. Therefore, zeta potential and NMR results demonstrate that FCQDs can adsorb zinc ions and H2O molecules, and thus new solvated clusters [FCQDsZnx(H2O)y]2x + can be formed.

Subsequently, density functional theory (DFT) calculation was conducted to further study the interaction between Zn2+ and FCQDs. As illustrated in Fig. 3f and Table S1, the calculated adsorption energies of Zn2+ with -COO- (−30.74 eV) and –SO3 - (−30.29 eV) on FCQDs are much higher than the weak adsorption between Zn2+ and H2O molecules (−16.23 eV). Additionally, the adsorption energy of FCQDs on Zn surface is higher than Zn atom on surface (Fig. S6), indicating those FCQDs are more easily absorbed on Zn surface. In order to demonstrate the distribution of charges on surface of FCQDs, the molecular electrostatic potential (MESP) mapping was calculated, as shown in Fig. 3g. The O atom in -COO- and –SO3 - groups exhibits a negative MESP (red region), which tend to appeal Zn2+ and surrounding H2O molecules, thus forming new ion aggregations [38,39]. Additionally, the electron transfer from Zn to O can be illustrated by the differential charge density, further verifying the strong interaction between Zn2+ and FCQDs (Fig. 3h). The strong adsorption of Zn2+ and water molecules with FCQDs generates the solvated FCQDs clusters [FCQDsZnx(H2O)y]2x+. To further explore the effect of FCQDs on Zn2+, the electric field distribution and Zn2+ ion flux simulations based on COMSOL near the Zn surface were carried out. For bare Zn (Fig. 3i (left)), the electric field tends to accumulate on these humps due to “tip effect”, which gives rise to the dendrite growth [40]. As shown in Fig. 3i (right), the presence of FCQDs can weaken “tip effect” and equalize the electric field distribution Zn surface, which is conducive to the uniform Zn deposition and reversible striping/plating process. And the more concentrated electric field strength can promote the Zn2+ diffusion through the FCQDs. Additionally, benefiting from these FCQDs, the Zn2+ flux near Zn electrode surface is more homogeneous compared with the pure ZnSO4 case (Fig. 3j), which can reduce the concentration polarization to achieve uniform Zn deposition [41,42]. Hence, the introduction of FCQDs can also redistribute the electric field and Zn2+ flux, thus contributing to dendrite-free behavior and uniform Zn deposition.

The compound electrolytes with different concentrations of FCQDs were tested to get the optimal concentration by Zn symmetric cells (Fig. S7). And the results disclose that 0.01 M is superior to other concentrations. Simultaneously, the ionic conductivity test was executed to validate the excellent electrochemical property of electrolyte with FCQDs (Fig. S8). The chronoamperometry (CA) test was performed to study nucleation process on Zn anode in symmetric batteries. It can be observed that the current in ZnSO4 electrolyte increases continuously, indicative of a long and rampant 2D diffusion process, which can give rise to the growth of dendrites (Fig. 4 a). In contrast, the FCQDs can absorb Zn2+ ions and H2O molecules, thus forming a special solvation structure [FCQDsZnx(H2O)y]2x+. Thereby, the 2D diffusion only lasts 140 s, and then turns to continuous 3D diffusion Zn accumulation for Zn2+ uniform deposition [43]. Besides, Tafel polarization curve was used to evaluate the Zn corrosion behavior (Fig. 4b). Apparently, with the aid of FCQDs, Zn anode exhibits a lower corrosion current density (1.08 mA cm−2) and higher corrosion potential (−0.959 V), whereas the Zn anode in pure ZnSO4 electrolyte exhibits a higher corrosion current density (3.34 mA cm−2) and lower corrosion potential (−0.975 V), demonstrating better anti-corrosion ability of the compound electrolyte with FCQDs [44]. To explore the effect of FCQDs on Zn nucleation behavior, the nucleation overpotential of Zn2+ in Zn||Cu asymmetric batteries were tested (Fig. 4c). Electrolyte with FCQDs exhibits a lower nucleation overpotential (29.6 mV) at 1 mA cm−2 than that in ZnSO4 electrolyte (38.5 mV), which means the lower nucleation barrier and facilitated Zn deposition process [45].

The CV tests of Zn||Cu asymmetric cells were performed to further corroborate the influence of FCQDs on Zn deposition/dissolution process. As illustrated in Fig. S9, the lower oxidation/reduction potentials in electrolyte with FCQDs indicate diminished polarization. And the higher intensity of redox peak manifests the higher reaction kinetics. Additionally, in the compound electrolyte with FCQDs, the reduction peak shifts positively while the oxidation peak shifts negatively, indicating the better reversibility. To investigate the striping/depositing stability of different electrolyte, CEs were provided as shown in Fig. S10. As for ZnSO4 electrolyte, the voltages of the Zn||Cu cells can maintain stable for only 75 cycles before dramatic fluctuation and its CE is not stable. In comparison, the voltage of the Zn||Cu cells using electrolyte with FCQDs can keep stable for 500 cycles, and its average CE is up to 98.91 % (Fig. S11). Subsequently, the Zn||Cu asymmetric cell was tested for 10 cycles at 1 mA cm−2. The asymmetric cell using electrolyte with FCQDs still showcased excellent cycling performance and delivered a high average CE of 97.83%, while the asymmetric cell using ZnSO4 electrolyte only exhibits a lower average CE of 95.69% (Fig. S12), which discloses that both the side reactions and dendrite growth are suppressed effectively by solvated FCQDs clusters [FCQDsZnx(H2O)y]2x+.

Furthermore, the FCQDs plays a significant role in the modulating Zn nucleation process. Fig. 4d shows the rate performance of their symmetric cells at various current densities from 1.0 to 20.0 mA cm−2 with a fixed capacity of 1.0 mAh cm−2. It is worth noting that the Zn symmetric cell using compound electrolyte with FCQDs always exhibits lower voltage hysteresis than that using ZnSO4 electrolyte at different current density, especially at high current density, suggesting the excellent rate performance of compound electrolyte with FCQDs (Fig. S13). As displayed in Fig. 4e, limit current density tests were performed, gradually increasing the current density (0.1 mA cm−2 for every cycle) with a fixed charging and discharging time (1h). For the ZnSO4 electrolyte, the voltage hysteresis increases gradually and a soft short circuit occurred when the current density is up to 1.6 cm−2. By comparison, the compound electrolytes can withstand higher current densities. The significantly enhanced performance is derived from the inhabitation of dendrite growth and side reactions by the new solvation structure of [FCQDsZnx(H2O)y]2x+.

These solvated clusters [FCQDsZnx(H2O)y]2x + endow the Zn||Zn symmetric cells with better cycling performance. As presented in Fig. 4f, for the symmetric Zn cell using pure ZnSO4 electrolyte, a short circuit occurs when cycled to about 170 h, probably attributed to the uncontrollable dendrite growth during cycling process. When cycled in compound electrolyte with FCQDs, the symmetric cell can steadily operate for 1520 h at 1 mA cm−2, surpassing the performance of ZnSO4 electrolyte. Even at high current density (10 mA cm−2, 1mAh cm−2), the Zn symmetric cell using electrolyte with FCQDs still exbibits longer cycling life with lower hysteresis than the case using ZnSO4 electrolyte (Fig. S14). Additionally, in the compound electrolyte with FCQDs, the Zn||Zn symmetric cell utilizing electrolyte with FCQDs delivers a stable cycling performance for 360 h under 28% DODZn, surpassing the ZnSO4 electrolyte (Fig. S15). Since the de-solvation energy of hydrated Zn2+ can hinder the Zn2+ migration during cycling. Arhenius activation energy (E
a ) was calculated to evaluate the de-solvation energy barrier of ZnSO4 electrolyte and compound electrolyte with FCQDs. As shown in Fig. 4g, the E
a of compound electrolyte with FCQDs (41.36 kJ mol−1) is much smaller than ZnSO4 electrolyte (52.35 kJ mol−1), which indicates that the FCQDs could effectively disassociate hydrated Zn2+ and form a new solvation structure [FCQDsZnx(H2O)y]2x + to decrease its de-solvation energy [46], thus improving the ion migration. The corresponding EIS curves of ZnSO4 electrolyte and compound electrolyte with FCQDs from 15 to 55 °C are provided in Fig. S16.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Dendrites and by-products investigation of Zn electrode with different electrolytes
ce:section.ce:para: SEM images of Zn anode of symmetric batteries using ZnSO4 electrolyte and compound electrolyte with FCQDs after different cycles (1 mA cm−2, 1mAh cm−2) were provided to attest the deposition condition. The uneven deposition on Zn anode using ZnSO4 electrolyte can be observed after first cycle (Fig. S17), while the surface of Zn anode using compound electrolyte with FCQDs is flat and compact. And the signal of sulfur element of Zn anode using ZnSO4 electrolyte is much stronger than that using electrolyte with FCQDs, which means more by-products. When the cycle number increased to 50, the Zn surface is covered with zinc flakes due to inhomogeneous deposition (Fig. 5 a). And these humps are more likely to attract Zn2+ ions due to “tip effect”, which may expedite the formation of dendrites and pierce through the separator finally [47]. By contrast, the electrolyte with FCQDs involved Zn deposition is still smooth and compact (Fig. 5b). Subsequently, the roughness and morphology of Zn anodes utilizing electrolyte with and without FCQDs were analyzed with AFM height test. The cycled Zn anode using 2 M ZnSO4 electrolyte showcases rough surface with average height of 270 nm after first cycle and 370 nm after 50 cycles (Fig. S18 and Fig. 5c). While the Zn anode based on electrolyte with FCQDs exhibits a relatively even surface with the average height as low as 115 nm after first cycle and 170 nm after 50 cycles (Fig. 5d). Besides, the FCQDs can remarkably affect the Zn plating behavior, as proved by in-situ microscope observation (Fig. S19). As for pure ZnSO4 electrolyte, some small tips appeared on Zn surface after 10 min plating at 10 mA cm−2 due to inhomogeneous Zn deposition. And Zn2+ ions tend to accumulate on these tips owing to the “tip effect” and the growth of dendrites can be accelerated. In comparison, the Zn interface in compound electrolyte maintains relatively uniform and compact, which intuitively proves the FCQDs can restrain the growth of dendrites.

In addition to dendrite inhabitation, the lifespan of ZIBs can also be prolonged by suppressing side reactions. To evaluate the suppression effect of compound electrolyte with FCQDs on side reaction, the Zn anodes after 50 cycles in different electrolytes were characterized by X-Ray Diffraction (XRD) measurements (Fig. 5e), respectively. In the pure ZnSO4 electrolyte, several new peaks representing Zn4SO4(OH)6•4H2O by-product (PDF#44–673) can be easily observed. The formation of Zn4SO4(OH)6•4H2O is stemmed from the side reaction between Zn metal and ZnSO4 electrolyte. In sharp comparison, almost no distinct diffraction peak of Zn4SO4(OH)6•4H2O is detected on the Zn electrode in compound electrolyte with FCQDs, indicating that the parasitic side reactions are suppressed successfully. The constraint of the side reactions is mainly attributed to the formation of new solvation structure [FCQDsZnx(H2O)y]2x+. And the de-solvation of [FCQDsZnx(H2O)y]2x + facilitated by FCQDs could also reduce the amount of active H2O molecules, thus contributing to the suppression of hydrogen evolution corrosion [48]. Furthermore, the Raman spectra also proves that compound electrolyte with FCQDs can effectively restrain side reactions since the peak representing by-products at 967 cm−1 could hardly be observed [49]. Nevertheless, as for ZnSO4 electrolyte, the peak of by-products is noticeable (Fig. 5f). And the corresponding Raman mapping image (collected at 967 cm−1, S–O group signal) demonstrates less by-products compared with ZnSO4 electrolyte (Fig. 5g–h).

To profoundly investigate the intrinsic function mechanism of FCQDs, the co-deposition of FCQDs and Zn atoms was proved by Ar+ etched XPS spectra illustrated in Fig. 5i. The C 1s spectrum exhibit four distinct peaks, ascribing to CC, C–O, CO and C–S, respectively, which is in accordance with the XPS spectra of FCQDs in Fig. 2f. And the co-deposition of Zn atoms and FCQDs can be proven by the FTIR spectrum of cycled Zn anode (Fig. S20). The typical group signals of FCQDs can be detected in cycled Zn anode, indicating the fact of co-deposition of Zn atoms and FCQDs. Additionally, the SEM image of co-deposition layer is provided in Fig. S21. And the corresponding element mapping images present obvious carbon signal in the deposition layer, thus proving the co-deposition of Zn atoms and FCQDs. Furthermore, the co-deposition of Zn atoms and FCQDs can also be substantiated by Spherical Aberration Corrected Transmission Electron Microscope (AC-TEM) image shown in Fig. 5j and the element distribution can be detected by corresponding EDS mapping (Fig. 5k). As can be seen that carbon element of FCQDs is evenly distributed. XPS spectra and AC-TEM images all confirm that FCQDs could co-deposit with Zn atoms.

To prove the practicality and superiority of the compound electrolyte with FCQDs, full Zn-based batteries are constructed by choosing commercial MnO2 as cathode and the corresponding XRD pattern and SEM images can be seen in Fig. S22 and Fig. S23. As shown in Fig. 6 a and 6b, after resting for 48 h, more than 92 % of the original capacity is retained and 1.49 V of open-circuit voltage is kept in the full battery using electrolyte with FCQDs, surpassing the 78.79 % and 1.43 V for FCQDs-free cell. In the CV curves at 1 mV s−1 (Fig. 6c), the alike redox peaks of the represent the reactions with H+/Zn2+ (de)intercalation during the charging/discharging process [50]. Interestingly, a new peak appearing at 1.1 V corresponds to the insertion of FCQDs, which can be manifested by the FTIR spectra (Fig. S24). Two obvious cathodic peaks corresponding to the Zn2+ insertion into MnO2 and reduction of Mn4+ to lower oxidation states could be observed. And the FCQDs can reduce polarization, which can be demonstrated by the lower oxidation peak (1.6 V) compared with that in pure ZnSO4 electrolyte (1.616 V). The compound electrolyte with FCQDs also renders the full batteries to convey a superior rate capability at current densities ranging from 0.1 to 2 A g−1 than that using ZnSO4 electrolyte (Fig. 6d), indicating the faster reaction kinetics.

Besides, the long-term cycling stability curves at the high current density of 1 A g−1 were plotted in Fig. 6e. The increased specific capacity in initial stage mainly due to the activation of cathode material. The Zn||MnO2 battery using ZnSO4 electrolyte presents rapid capacity degradation as the charging and discharging proceeds, decaying to only 53.02 mAh g−1 after 340 cycles, with a capacity retention of 34%. While a high capacity of 103.94 mAh g−1 is achieved for the full cell using electrolyte with FCQDs after 1000 cycles at 1 A g−1. And the CE is consistently above 99% throughout the cycling. Relatively similar galvanostatic discharge profiles with two sloping plateaus corresponding to proton intercalation at around 1.45 V and Zn2+ intercalation at around 1.3 V were recorded shown in Fig. 6f. Furthermore, the full battery using compound electrolyte with FCQDs exhibits much better cycling stability compared with ZnSO4 electrolyte. Typically, the initial capacity increase is mainly ascribed to the cathode material activation process [51]. The cycling performance of full battery utilizing compound electrolyte with the MnO2 loading of 9.2 mg cm−2 at 0.2 A g−1 showcased great cycling stability with a capacity retention of 70.8% after 600 cycles (Fig. 6g). In contrast, rapid capacity decay occurred in the full cell using pure ZnSO4 electrolyte. In addition, the practicability of Zn||MnO2 full battery using compound electrolyte with FCQDs is also demonstrated as displayed in Fig. 6h. The voltage of Zn||MnO2 battery can reach up to 5.2 V by three pouch batteries in series with a size of 4 cm × 4 cm, which can light up a LIBs driven LED array (replacing the original LIBs) [52]. Therefore, we firmly believe that the full batteries using compound electrolyte with FCQDs have fascinating potential to serve as practical energy storage devices.

In conclusion, a simple and effective electrolyte optimization strategy based on hybrid compound electrolyte with FCQDs was successfully designed and demonstrated to effectively suppress dendrite growth and side reactions. With abundant negatively charged groups, the FCQDs are capable of regulating the Zn2+ solvation structure via strongly interacting with Zn2+ ions. The solvated clusters ([FCQDsZnx(H2O)y]2x+) can decrease the overpotential of nucleation and guide the uniform Zn deposition through the co-deposition of FCQDs and Zn2+ ions. Additionally, the unique FCQDs solvation structure can also decrease the de-solvation energy and thus suppressing HER and related by-products. With those merits, the Zn symmetric battery using compound electrolyte with FCQDs exhibits a good cycling performance for more than 1500 h at 1 mA cm−2 and 1 mAh cm−2. The assembled full cell with a loading of 9.2 mg cm−2 can deliver a high specific capacity of 125 mAh g−1, which proves its potential for practical application. The in-situ constructed solvated FCQDs clusters provide a simple and effective way to suppress the growth of dendrites and realize stable Zn anode.

Dengke Wang: Writing – original draft, Software, Methodology, Investigation, Conceptualization. Danyang Zhao: Software, Methodology, Investigation. Mingjun Chen: Visualization, Supervision, Methodology. Haoyuan Zheng: Software, Methodology, Formal analysis. Jiaqiang Yu: Software, Investigation. Xixian Qin: Validation, Software. Wenming Zhang: Resources, Project administration. Qiancheng Zhu: Writing – review & editing, Resources, Project administration, Methodology, Funding acquisition, Conceptualization.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by Nature Science Foundation of Hebei Province E2022201017 and the Advanced Talents Incubation Program of the Hebei University No. 521100221039.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: T. Xiong, Z. G. Yu, H. Wu, Y. Du, Q. Xie, J. Chen, Y. Zhang, S. J. Pennycook, W. S. V. Lee, Defect Engineering of Oxygen-Deficient Manganese Oxide to Achieve High-Performing Aqueous Zinc Ion Battery. Adv. Energy Mater. 9 (14) (2019) 1803815.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Unlocking the capacity of vanadium oxide by atomically thin graphene-analogous V2O5·nH2O in aqueous zinc-ion batteries

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: D. Zhao, X. Wang, W. Zhang, Y. Zhang, Y. Lei, X. Huang, W. Zhang, Q. Zhu, Unlocking the Capacity of Vanadium Oxide by Atomically Thin Graphene-Analogous V2O5·nH2O in Aqueous Zinc-Ion Batteries. Adv. Funct. Mater. 33 (13) (2022) 2211412 .
